request.question: What nationality is the only athlete who has won both Senior All-Ireland Championship medal and this contest in 2005 in Melbourne attended by 91,898 spectators.

title: Tadhg Kennelly
Kerry GAA honours
Emer Dillon
David Curtin
Rachel Moloney
Mark Davoren
Ireland National League (rugby league)
2005 AFL Grand Final
Jenny Duffy
Brian Farrell (Gaelic footballer)
sentences: Tadhg Kennelly (born 1 July 1981) is an Irish sportsperson known for his top level careers in both Gaelic football and Australian rules football.
He is the only holder of both an AFL Premiership medallion and a Senior All-Ireland Championship medal, the highest possible achievement in both sports.
He has also represented Ireland in the International Rules Series.
Kerry are by far the most successful team in the history of Gaelic football.
They have won 37 All-Ireland Senior Football Championship and have appeared in countless other All-Ireland Football Finals.
Kerry footballers have been garlanded with countless awards and hold numerous individual records in the sport.

Pat Spillane received nine All Stars during a glittering career, a feat matched by no other Gaelic footballer, while Tadhg Kennelly is the only holder of both an AFL Premiership medallion and a Senior All-Ireland Championship medal, the highest possible achievement in the sports of Australian rules football and Gaelic football.
Here are Kerry's honours.
Emer Dillon born 1984 in Cork is a camogie player and a marketing executive, winner of All Ireland camogie medals in 2002, 2005, 2006, and 2009 and a camogie All Star in 2005.
Emer was selected as player of the match in the 2005 All-Ireland Senior final.
She is also a prominent hockey player and has represented both Munster and Ireland.
She holds a Senior county championship medal with Carrigdhoun and also Junior and under-age championship honours with Ballygarvan.
She has won All-Ireland Senior, Intermediate and Minor medals as well as inter-provincial honours but did not participate in the 2008 championship due to study.
David Curtin (born 13 September 1980) is an inter-county hurling player who plays for Dublin.
He plays his club hurling for Dublin side Ballyboden St. Enda's.
In 2005, he will be remembered for his astounding performance against Laois which confirmed Dublins All-Ireland status for the 2006 championship.
Curtin finished the game with an impressive scoreline of 1-7.
In the 2006 All-Ireland championship, this feat was repeated when Dublin swept aside Westmeath to once retain their Liam McCarthy Cup status.
He finished the 2006 championship with a scoreline of 0-33, a scoreline which left him 5th of the top scorer list despite Dublins lack of progress in the All-Ireland championship.
Curtin was the Dublin hurling captain for 2005.
David was named on the 2006 Dublin Bus/Evening Herald Blue Star hurling XV at Centre Forward.
Curtin won his first Dublin Senior Hurling Championship medal with his club Ballyboden St Endas in October 2007.
Since then Boden have won 4 titles in a row.
The 2007 Dublin championship win qualified Ballyboden to play in their first ever Leinster Senior Club Hurling Championship game against Oulart the Ballagh GAA of Wexford.
Boden won the game by 1-17 to 0-15 with Curtin scoring a total of 0-04.
Rachel Moloney born 1982 in Cork is a camogie player and a marketing executive, winner of All Ireland camogie medals in 2008, and 2009 and camogie All Star awards in 2005, 2008 and 2009.
She holds Ashbourne Cup medals with U.C.C. as well as Minor, Junior, Intermediate and Senior All-Ireland championship honours with Cork.
Has won inter-provincial honours with Munster and is the side's free-taker and top scorer.
Rachel is coach to her club's Under-14 team.
Mark Davoren is an Irish Gaelic footballer for Dublin and Kilmacud Crokes.
He made his debut for Dublin against Kerry in the National Football League (Ireland) in 2009 scoring a total of 1-03 in the game which finished as a draw in Parnell Park.
He won an All-Ireland Senior Club Football Championship medal with Kilmacud Crokes on St Patricks Day 2009 in Croke Park against Crosmaglen Rangers.
He scored a goal in the game.
Davoren won a Leinster Senior Club Football Championship medal with Crokes against Rhode of Offalyin 2008 at Parnell Park, he scored 1-00 in the final.
He won the Dublin Senior Football Championship in 2008 by beating St Oliver Plunketts/Eoghan Ruadh in Parnell Park scoring a goal in the process.
He won an AFL Dublin Division One title with Kilmacud Crokes in 2008, although he did not make an appearance in the league final.
The All-Ireland Rugby League Championship (now the McGettigans All-Ireland League for sponsorship reasons) was first played for in 1997 under the title All-Ireland Challenge Cup.
Since then it has also been known as the All-Ireland League, Elite League and the All-Ireland Championship.
The 2005 AFL Grand Final was an Australian rules football game contested between the Sydney Swans and West Coast Eagles at the Melbourne Cricket Ground on 24 September 2005.
It was the 109th annual Grand Final of the Australian Football League (formerly the Victorian Football League), staged to determine the premiers for the 2005 AFL season.
The match, attended by 91,898 spectators, was won by Sydney by a margin of four points, marking the club's fourth Premiership and their first since 1933.
Jennifer "Jenny" Duffy (born 1983 in Cork) is a camogie player, footballer and camogie development officer, winner of All Ireland camogie medals in 2009.
A former student at Cork IT, Jenny is the Camogie Regional Development officer in South Leinster.
She is the holder of All-Ireland championship and league medals with CIT as well as Senior and Junior All-Ireland championship medals and National League.
She won a Senior county championship with her club and captained CIT to the Ashbourne Shield title in 2006.
Brian Farrell is a Gaelic footballer from Nobber, County Meath, Ireland.
He plays with the Meath county team and his local club Nobber.

He first came to national attention when he was part of the Meath team that made it to the 2002 All-Ireland Minor Football Championship final but lost out to Derry having already been beaten by Longford in the Leinster Championship final.
The following year, he was part of the Meath side that won the Leinster and All-Ireland Junior Football Championships.
At club level, he has won County and Leinster Junior medals in 2002 and an All-Ireland Junior Club Football Championship medal in 2003.
In 2010, Farrell won a Meath Intermediate Football Championship medal.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Role: This agent summarizes first-hop evidence and exposes the bridge entity, any missing fact(s), ambiguity, and concrete next-hop retrieval directions. Do not attempt to produce the final answer—the summary should tell the next agent exactly what to fetch to finish the multi-hop chain.

Output requirements (single `summary` value only):
- Start the summary with the header: First-hop findings:
- Include these labeled parts (use a short bullet list or 2–4 concise sentences; keep overall output brief):
  1) Evidence found: For each person/entity named in the question, list which passage(s) mention matching candidate pages/titles and quote any explicit fact tokens found there (names, birth years, publication years, short parenthetical notes). Keep quoted fragments <=15 words and cite the passage title/label for each fact.
  2) Ambiguities: If a name yields multiple candidate pages or variants in the passages, explicitly enumerate those candidate titles/labels as they appear; if an expected page/variant is missing, note that too.
  3) Bridge entity / Missing fact: Explicitly name the bridge entity exactly as it appears in the passages (the person, work, or organization that links to the next hop) and state the precise missing fact needed to answer the question (e.g., a birth date, founding year, canonical series ordering).
  4) Retrieval direction: Provide 2–4 concrete, prioritized next-hop targets (specific page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, and/or exact sections to check such as lede/infobox/Career/History). Include short example search keywords or one-line instructions (e.g., "open '<Name>' page and check lede/infobox and 'Career' section for 'Italian champion' year").
- End with a brief explicit note that the final answer is deferred until those pages are retrieved.

Hard constraints (do not violate):
- Do not provide a final comparison or answer here. Do not guess or invent facts not present in the provided passages or introduce outside knowledge.
- Do not assume which candidate is intended when multiple candidates exist.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passages (>15 words).
- Preserve concise factual phrasing and verbatim tokens that appear in the passages (names, dates, page titles) when reporting evidence.

Keep the summary concise and focused—just the first-hop findings needed to guide the next retrieval hop.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Output contract:
- Produce exactly one field named `query` whose value is a single short retrieval query string (one line, no surrounding explanation or extra fields).
- Do not answer, summarize, or explain anything in `query`. Do not include fallback instructions or user-facing requests for more info in this field.

Construction rules (apply the most specific rule that fits the inputs):
1) Linking two named works: If `question` or `summary_1` names two or more specific works (songs, tracks, titles) and the missing evidence is the bridge between them, emit a concise interrogative that names both works and requests the linking evidence (artist / album / recording). Keep it short (about ≤ 12 words). Example form: "Which artist or album recorded both 'Work A' and 'Work B'?"
2) Missing album artist/genre: If `summary_1` indicates the album title is present but the artist or genre is unknown, emit a compact search string of the form: <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
   - No punctuation, no question phrasing, no extra words. Example: "Levitate album artist genre"
3) Sports season-year lookups: If the `question` contains a specific season-year token and the missing bridge is a league-season page, emit a short query that includes the year token, a league identifier (e.g., "NHL" or "National Hockey League"), and a cue such as "regular season" or "season count". Keep it concise (≤ ~10 tokens).
4) Default: Otherwise, produce one concise search-style query (≤ ~12 words) that uses visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` targeted at retrieving the missing supporting evidence (artist, album, recording, genre, season page, etc.).

General guidance:
- Keep queries compact; avoid lists, long page titles, or copying large spans from hidden targets.
- Do not invent facts or candidate answers; use only visible tokens or neutral retrieval cues.
- Prefer a short search string; use an interrogative only for the two-works linking case in rule 1.
- The goal is to maximize recovery of the missing bridge evidence for the downstream retriever.

Return only the single `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Primary function: integrate the newly retrieved `passages` (second-hop evidence) with the provided `context` (first-hop) so the output is a grounded post-retrieval state that preserves the facts the final-answer generator needs. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when evidence is insufficient.

Behavior rules (minimal, general):
1) Preserve concise-answer behavior. If `context` already contains a concise direct answer (a short sentence or phrase), keep that text exactly as the first sentence of `summary`, unless it contradicts the `passages`.
2) When `passages` supply additional, directly supported facts about entities named in the `question` or `context`, append up to one brief supporting sentence per distinct named entity (keep total `summary` to 1–3 sentences, preferably 1–2). Each supporting sentence should: start with the entity's full name as shown in the passages, include a short paraphrased attribution drawn only from the passages (no more than ~10–12 words) such as nationality, a notable achievement, a birth date, or a current affiliation, and avoid inventing or inferring facts not present in the inputs.
3) When an object/entity page (for example, a car page, group page, or similar) lists associated people and a separate passage provides a biography for one of those listed people, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary: (a) first sentence: name the people as listed on the object page (e.g., "driven by X and Y" or "member of Group Z"); (b) optional second short clause/sentence: paraphrase key biographical facts from the person's passage (only facts present there — e.g., nationality, notable wins/awards, birth date, current team/affiliation). Keep wording compact and factual.
4) Ground everything in the provided `passages` and `context`. Do not add new claims, dates, roles, or affiliations not explicitly supported. Avoid long verbatim quotations from the passages; you may paraphrase short phrases (≤10 words) only if they appear in the sources, and you may copy short strings such as names or dates exactly as shown.
5) If the passages do not provide enough evidence to resolve the bridge, state that the passages do not specify the requested fact while still listing any supported names or facts. Do not guess or fabricate missing information.
6) Output contract: return only the field `summary` containing 1–3 concise sentences (preferably 1–2) that integrate the first-hop `context` and second-hop `passages` according to the rules above. Do not add commentary, answer-labels, or extraneous text.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only the visible summaries (summary_1 and summary_2) together with the question as your evidence. Do not introduce external facts, sources, or claims not present in those summaries.
- Treat summary_2 (the later, integrated post-retrieval state) as primary evidence while still considering summary_1. Do not assume the final state is unresolved simply because summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge; rely on the combined visible summaries to reach a conclusion.
- If summary_1 and summary_2 both clearly and unambiguously support the same binary (yes/no) conclusion to the question, then the value of the `answer` field must be exactly one lowercase token: either "yes" (if both summaries affirm the fact) or "no" (if both summaries deny the fact). The `answer` field must contain only that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no capitalization, no explanation, no additional words).
- If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, or do not together provide a clear binary yes/no verdict, produce a concise answer string in the `answer` field that best reflects what the summaries support. This concise answer may be a short phrase or sentence, but must still be supported solely by the summaries and must avoid adding new facts.
- Always minimize verbosity: prefer the single-token binary response when the summaries unambiguously agree; otherwise keep the answer brief and directly tied to the visible summaries.

Produce only the `answer` field as output.

response.hop1_query: What nationality is the only athlete who has won both Senior All-Ireland Championship medal and this contest in 2005 in Melbourne attended by 91,898 spectators.
hop1_docs: 2005 AFL Grand Final | The 2005 AFL Grand Final was an Australian rules football game contested between the Sydney Swans and West Coast Eagles at the Melbourne Cricket Ground on 24 September 2005.  It was the 109th annual Grand Final of the Australian Football League (formerly the Victorian Football League), staged to determine the premiers for the 2005 AFL season.  The match, attended by 91,898 spectators, was won by Sydney by a margin of four points, marking the club's fourth Premiership and their first since 1933.

Emer Dillon | Emer Dillon born 1984 in Cork is a camogie player and a marketing executive, winner of All Ireland camogie medals in 2002, 2005, 2006, and 2009 and a camogie All Star in 2005.  Emer was selected as player of the match in the 2005 All-Ireland Senior final.  She is also a prominent hockey player and has represented both Munster and Ireland.  She holds a Senior county championship medal with Carrigdhoun and also Junior and under-age championship honours with Ballygarvan.  She has won All-Ireland Senior, Intermediate and Minor medals as well as inter-provincial honours but did not participate in the 2008 championship due to study.

Cookstown Fr. Rock's GAC | Cookstown Father Rocks Gaelic Athletic Club is a Gaelic Athletic Association club based in Cookstown in County Tyrone, Northern Ireland.  In recent years Owen Mulligan and Raymond Mulgrew have both won All-Ireland Senior Football Championship medals with the Tyrone senior team.  The club also had five players who won All-Ireland Minor Football Championship medals in the same day in 2004.

2005 All-Ireland Senior Camogie Championship | The 2005 All-Ireland Senior Camogie Championship—known as the Foras na Gaeilge All-Ireland Senior Camogie Championship for sponsorship reasons—was the high point of the 2005 season in the sport of camogie.  The championship was won for the 21st time by Cork who defeated Tipperary by a four-point margin in the final and became part of the legendary “rebel treble” of 2005 when Cork won the senior hurling, camogie and ladies’ football titles.  The attendance was 14,350.

Ollie Murphy | Ollie Murphy is a Meath Footballer.  He plays club football for Carnaross.  He came to national prominence in 1999 when he was one of Meath's best player's as they won the All-Ireland Senior Football Championship that year, scoring the decisive goal in that year's All Ireland final.  This was his second All Ireland senior medal as he was a substitute when Meath won the 1996 All-Ireland Senior Football Championship.  He is also the holder of 3 Leinster Senior Football Championship medals.  Ollie has won 2 GAA All Stars Awards.  Murphys best season was the 2001 championship where he put in a number of superb display's as Meath reached another All Ireland final.  In that final he had to leave the field of play with a hand injury.  In recent years his form has been not up to what it once was and he has failed to warrant a starting position.  Ollie made his senior inter-county debut playing for London in the 1994 Connacht Championship against Galway.  It was his one and only appearance for the Exiles.  Murphy's Benny father was also an inter-county player, he lined out for his native Fermanagh

Aoife Murray | Aoife Murray (born 1984 in Dunmanway) is a Camogie player and quantity surveyor based in Cork, winner of All Ireland medals in 2002, 2005, 2006, 2008, 2009, 2014 and 2015.  All Star awards in 2004, 2008, 2009, 2012, 2014 and 2015.  Minor, Junior, Intermediate as well as her Senior All-Ireland medals and Munster championship honours in all grades.  Won three county Senior championship medals with Cloughduv and was captain in 2005 for their third county title.  Sister of former Cork Senior hurler, Kevin Murray, who won an All- Ireland medal in 1999.  A series of impressive saves in the 2009 All Ireland final won for her the "player of the match” award from RTÉ.

1989 All-Ireland Senior Camogie Championship | The 1989 All-Ireland Senior Camogie Championship was won by Killkenny who defeated Cork by an eight-point margin in the final.  The match drew an attendance of 3,024 and marked Angela Downey’s ninth All Ireland medal.
response.summary_1:
First-hop findings:
1) Evidence found:
- "2005 AFL Grand Final" — passage [1] explicitly: "2005 AFL Grand Final" and "attended by 91,898 spectators" (passage [1]).
- All‑Ireland Senior medals (candidate pages in the passages):
  - Emer Dillon: "winner of All Ireland camogie medals in 2002, 2005, 2006, and 2009" (passage [2]).
  - Aoife Murray: "winner of All Ireland medals in 2002, 2005, 2006, 2008, 2009…" (passage [6]).
  - Cookstown Fr. Rock's GAC page noting players: "Owen Mulligan and Raymond Mulgrew have both won All‑Ireland Senior Football Championship medals" (passage [3]).
  - Ollie Murphy: described as having "won the All‑Ireland Senior Football Championship" (passage [5]).
2) Ambiguities:
- "All‑Ireland Senior Championship medal" could refer to different sports (camogie vs. Gaelic football); passages include both camogie (Emer Dillon, Aoife Murray) and football (Owen Mulligan, Raymond Mulgrew, Ollie Murphy) candidates.
- No passage names any athlete who also won the 2005 AFL Grand Final; multiple candidate names exist but none are explicitly linked to the 2005 AFL match in the provided passages.
3) Bridge entity / Missing fact:
- Bridge entity (as titled in passages): "2005 AFL Grand Final".
- Precise missing fact needed: the name of the athlete who has both (a) an All‑Ireland Senior Championship medal (specifying which code: camogie or football) and (b) a winner of the 2005 AFL Grand Final — and that athlete's nationality.
4) Retrieval direction (prioritized):
- Open "2005 AFL Grand Final" page (passage [1]) and check the team lists/squads and player entries (lede/Teams/Match details) for players who are/are from Ireland. Search keywords: "2005 AFL Grand Final team list Sydney players Irish".
- Open the individual page "Tadhg Kennelly" (likely candidate) — check lede/infobox and Career/Honours for "2005 premiership" and any All‑Ireland Senior medal mention. Search keywords: "Tadhg Kennelly All‑Ireland".
- Fetch pages for named All‑Ireland winners from passages (Emer Dillon, Aoife Murray, Owen Mulligan, Ollie Murphy) and check ledes/infoboxes for any AFL premiership links. Search keywords: "<Name> AFL premiership" or "<Name> Sydney Swans 2005".
- If needed, fetch "Irish players in the Australian Football League" or "List of foreign-born AFL premiership players" pages and check sections listing premiership winners by nationality.
Final answer deferred until those pages (player pages and the 2005 AFL Grand Final team lists) are retrieved and show the overlapping athlete and their nationality.
response.hop2_query: Tadhg Kennelly All-Ireland 2005 AFL Grand Final nationality
hop2_docs: Noel Kennelly | Noel Kennelly is a footballer from the Listowel Emmets club in North Kerry.  He is a son of former Kerry great Tim Kennelly and older brother of Tadhg Kennelly.  He won an All Ireland Medal with Kerry in 2000.  He won an All Ireland Under 21 medal in 1998 and played in the losing final of 1999, he also played in the losing All Ireland Minor final in 1996.

Tadhg Kennelly | Tadhg Kennelly (born 1 July 1981) is an Irish sportsperson known for his top level careers in both Gaelic football and Australian rules football.  He is the only holder of both an AFL Premiership medallion and a Senior All-Ireland Championship medal, the highest possible achievement in both sports.  He has also represented Ireland in the International Rules Series.

Kerry GAA honours | Kerry are by far the most successful team in the history of Gaelic football.  They have won 37 All-Ireland Senior Football Championship and have appeared in countless other All-Ireland Football Finals.  Kerry footballers have been garlanded with countless awards and hold numerous individual records in the sport.  Pat Spillane received nine All Stars during a glittering career, a feat matched by no other Gaelic footballer, while Tadhg Kennelly is the only holder of both an AFL Premiership medallion and a Senior All-Ireland Championship medal, the highest possible achievement in the sports of Australian rules football and Gaelic football.  Here are Kerry's honours.

2005 AFL Grand Final | The 2005 AFL Grand Final was an Australian rules football game contested between the Sydney Swans and West Coast Eagles at the Melbourne Cricket Ground on 24 September 2005.  It was the 109th annual Grand Final of the Australian Football League (formerly the Victorian Football League), staged to determine the premiers for the 2005 AFL season.  The match, attended by 91,898 spectators, was won by Sydney by a margin of four points, marking the club's fourth Premiership and their first since 1933.

Listowel Emmets GAA | Listowel Emmets is a Gaelic Athletic Association club based in Listowel, County Kerry, Ireland.  They play in Division 3 of the County Football league and in the Kerry Intermediate Football Championship.  Famous players from the club include Tim Kennelly, and his 2 sons Tadhg and Noel and his famous brother Eamon.

2016 AFL Grand Final | The 2016 AFL Grand Final was an Australian rules football match contested between the Sydney Swans and the Western Bulldogs .  It was the 120th annual Grand Final of the Australian Football League (formerly the Victorian Football League), staged to determine the premiers of the 2016 AFL season.  It was the Swans' fifth grand final appearance since 2005, while the Bulldogs were competing in their first grand final since 1961.  The match, played at the Melbourne Cricket Ground in front of 99,981 spectators, was won by the Bulldogs by a margin of 22 points, marking their second VFL/AFL premiership victory and first since 1954, breaking a 62-year premiership drought.  It was also the first time in VFL/AFL history that a team won the premiership from seventh place on the ladder.  Jason Johannisen of the Bulldogs was awarded the Norm Smith Medal as the best player on the ground.

2005 AFL finals series | The Australian Football League's 2005 finals series began on the weekend of 2 September 2005 and ended with the 109th AFL Grand Final at the Melbourne Cricket Ground on 24 September 2005.  The Sydney Swans defeated West Coast, breaking their record 72-year drought between premierships.  The top eight teams on the home and away rounds (regular season) ladder qualify for the Finals Series.
response.summary_2: Irish. Tadhg Kennelly is an Irish sportsperson and the only holder of both an AFL Premiership medallion and a Senior All‑Ireland Championship medal.
